name: Michael Soetaert
occupation: Art gallery owner, former Wellington city council member
family: Adopted at birth into a Roman Catholic family; descendant of Constant Soetaert; adoptive father was Vice President of Coca-Cola Mid-America and former NASA Mission Control staff for the first 16 Apollo missions
whoTheyAre:
Michael Soetaert is a Wellington, Kansas, business owner and the first openly gay person to run for U.S. Senate in Kansas history. He grew up in Spring Hill, Kansas, was adopted at birth into a Roman Catholic family, and comes from a long Kansas lineage -- he is a direct descendant of Constant Soetaert and knew his great-grandfather Charles as a child. His adoptive father served in NASA Mission Control for the first 16 Apollo missions before becoming a vice president at Coca-Cola Mid-America. The Soetaert family is well known in Wellington, where former Mayor Tony Soetaert is a relative.

Soetaert was formerly a Republican who described himself as "conservative" and "pro-life" to the Hutchinson News as recently as 2019. He says the COVID-19 pandemic, January 6, and his experience as a gay man in the Republican Party drove him to switch to the Democratic Party. He changed his position on abortion after Kansas voters rejected an anti-abortion constitutional amendment in August 2022, saying "Kansans voted for bodily autonomy." He also publicly backed the federal Respect for Marriage Act, which protects same-sex marriage.

His campaign operates on a very small budget. His website is hosted on Canva's free website builder, and he has used multiple domain names for campaigns over the years, including replacejerrymoran.org, mike4kansas.org, and mikeforkansas.net. He is running in a crowded Democratic primary with seven other candidates.
theirRecord:
Soetaert's political record is defined more by the number of campaigns he has run than by accomplishments in office. He ran for Council Grove City Council in 2019, the Republican primary for Kansas's 1st Congressional District in 2020, the U.S. Senate Democratic primary in 2022, the Kansas State Senate District 32 race in 2024, and even mounted a bid for President of the United States in 2024. None of those campaigns succeeded. In November 2025, he won a seat on the Wellington City Council with 962 votes, making it his first electoral victory.

However, Soetaert resigned from that city council seat on December 12, 2025, just nine days after being inducted and after attending only two meetings. The council accepted his resignation 5-0 with one abstention, and Jerry Elmore Jr. was appointed to fill his vacant seat. Soetaert left the council to file for the 2026 U.S. Senate race, making this his fourth attempt at federal office.

His brief tenure on the Wellington City Council is the full extent of his record in elected office. He has no legislative voting record at the state or federal level, no major policy achievements, and no known endorsements from elected officials or political organizations. His supporters point to his historic candidacy and his willingness to keep running as evidence of persistence. His critics say the pattern of running for progressively higher offices while being unable to hold the one seat he won raises serious questions about his commitment to governing.
whatYouShouldKnow: Soetaert is the first openly gay person to run for U.S. Senate in Kansas history.

He has run for at least six different offices since 2019, including city council, U.S. House, U.S. Senate (twice), Kansas State Senate, and President. His only win was a Wellington City Council seat in November 2025, which he resigned after nine days and two meetings to run for U.S. Senate.

He was a registered Republican who called himself conservative and pro-life as recently as 2019. He switched to the Democratic Party after the pandemic, January 6, and conflicts over LGBTQ issues within the GOP.

He changed his position on abortion after the 2022 Kansas referendum, in which voters rejected a constitutional amendment that would have removed abortion protections. He now supports reproductive rights.
He publicly supports the Equality Act and the Respect for Marriage Act, which provide legal protections for same-sex couples.

His campaign website is hosted on Canva's free platform, and he has no known major donors, endorsements, or campaign staff. This is a very lean operation compared to the other seven Democrats in the primary.
He was not named in the March 2026 NRSC ethics complaint that targeted four other Democratic Senate candidates for late financial disclosure filings.
His adoptive father worked in NASA Mission Control for the first 16 Apollo missions. His family includes former Wellington Mayor Tony Soetaert.
whereTheyWorship: Soetaert was adopted at birth into what he has described as a "right wing, fundamentalist, Roman Catholic family." He publicly identifies as Christian, but no current church membership, congregation, or denomination has been verified in any public reporting or campaign materials. His campaign website and social media do not name a church or include faith-based messaging beyond the general Christian identification.

quotes.text: If I had to choose between your vote and your health and safety, this is easy. I pick your health and safety. I would love to represent you and the great state of Kansas in the U.S. Senate. However, I'm not willing to walk over your body bag, coffin or funereal urn to do so.
It really is an online campaign, a pandemic campaign, and I don't see that changing between now and November.
No. 1, the mismanagement of the pandemic under the Republican party was in and of itself reason enough to go home to the Democratic party.

campaignFinance.narrative: Soetaert has filed with the Federal Election Commission under candidate ID S2KS00097, but the accessible public summary does not display clean 2025-2026 fundraising totals. His primary public fundraising channel is an ActBlue donation page. The campaign's use of a free Canva-hosted website, the absence of visible campaign staff, and the lack of reported major donors all point to a very small-budget operation. He was notably not named in the March 2026 NRSC complaint about late financial disclosure filings, which targeted four other Democratic candidates in the race.
campaignFinance.undisclosed: No individual donor information is publicly available. The campaign's financial footprint appears minimal.
campaignFinance.reportingPeriod: 2025-2026 cycle (totals not yet displayed in public FEC filings)
